All right, your back, let's rock on it. Let's go right through this now. Moving on to number four. This is a really big one. Need to use your gifts correctly. What do I mean by that? Well, let me put it this way. One of the most destructive things you can do for a new client, especially selling B2B, is to give them a gift that has absolutely no value to them. So you know what it shows that you really don't care about them. Number two, that you're just trying to bribe them, which is not a great thing. And number three, it just poisons everything going forward. Because now I start looking, why does this guy have to give me something as a gift? Is he overcharging? What's the other side of the equation? In other words, here's a perfect example of how it happened to me. Back in the day when I owned Stratton, right, this immense brokerage firm, I had a massive monthly phone bill, about a half a million dollars a month. This is before, you know, over the internet calling, right, you had a long distance carrier and all these other carriers were constantly trying to bribe me to get my business, right? Well, one day they sent me four tickets to a baseball game, all right? I think it was like a playoff of something, right? Now here's the deal. I hate baseball. You know, offense, I just don't like watching baseball. They sent me these four tickets. They have zero value to me. Not only that, I'm like, oh, they're trying to give me tickets. I bet you their prices are high. They're the other guys, right? That's why they get money to give me the tickets. They're trying to bribe me to get my business. Now think this through though. If they would have given me tickets to the US Open to watch, and I know this is a different time of Federer and the Doll went around back then, but let's just say to watch Federer and the Doll, huh, well that would have been a very different story, okay? In other words, they were on the 10 back then, those guys. My point is that would have said, whoa, I mean, these guys know what I care about. So it means they care about me. They take the time to research my wants and desires, and they went out and got me these tickets. What a nice gesture. I'd be really, really impressed. On the other side of the equation though, okay, you use gifts incorrectly. It can be another example. Once they dropped off this gigantic 40, now this is before flat screens, right, in the olden days, right? It was a 42 inch box, it's old fashioned, big TVs. It was so big, it was like a thousand pounds, it took up half my office. It was the royal pain in the butt for me, right? And every time I looked through this thing, I'm like, gosh, these bastards look like that. Those bastards, they dropped this TV. I almost didn't want to do business with them again. You get it? So it could be really, really dangerous to giving someone a gift that's meaningless to them, okay? Now that being said, there's another danger here with giving gifts, and that is to give someone a gift that's tied directly to what they do for a living. For example, what's the worst gift you could give to a plumber? What would that be? A wrench. You get it? You know why? You don't want to give a plumber a wrench to try to buy his loyalty, all right? That all it does is it reminds him of what he does for a living. So let's say you're a supplier and you're selling to plumbers, don't be giving them tools, right? You want to find out what the plumber likes to do when they're not plumbing. Do they like to fish, hunt, go bowling, whatever it might be? You want to identify what their aliveness is, what makes them feel alive out of work. So this is what your expanded, post-closing intelligence gathering is all about, right? In other words, while we would never go off the Pluto and talk about things that aren't germane to the sale during our primary intelligence gathering phase, right? I said this last lesson, well, guess what? Now we are in that post-closing arena where we want to know what makes them tick outside of work, identifying their aliveness, what makes them feel alive. So what I do, let's say they like fishing or hunting, well, a great gift would be a fishing or hunting magazine that shows up in their mailbox every single month. Now I know nowadays people look on the internet, maybe it could be a subscription they get on the internet where every single month they get an email saying, here's your monthly subscription, how great would that be if they get an email each month coming from the magazine company, but on behalf of you, they get reminded every single month that you've given them a gift that makes them feel good about life, not plumbing. That is really awesome versus just reminding them what they have to do for a living to make money to live the life they want. It actually turns the person off. So again, you want to be very, very careful with your giving of gifts. Give the right gift, it'll help you sort of success. Give the wrong gift, well, it might not destroy you, but it's certainly not going to give you those brownie points, it'll help that reciprocity and that goodwill that will help drive your business forward. Now real quick, just to sum all the subtleties, just give me a six point this year, actually maybe even seven here, just to think about why you're giving gifts. Summs it up. Now number one, make sure that the gift that you give relates to the person's personal life, not their business life. Number one, number two, make sure that that gift actually matters to them, meaning gather that post-closing intelligence and then use that information to decide what gift you should give. Number three, whenever possible, make this gift as visible as possible. For example, if I could choose to have a gift when they got an email in their inbox each month, great. Or how about this gift? How about someone that loves taking trips to different parts of the world to eat at restaurants or the sightseeing? How about a living room table book that plops down on their table right there that everyone could see right out in the forefront. You get it? That's in their line of sight. Has to what they love. So that would be just a great example of something that they see and that's going to work. Now number four, and I mentioned this before again, things that have recurring monthly subscriptions, whatever they might be. In other words, every single month the person is reminded that you gave them a gift that has value. It either shows up in their inbox as I said. It could be something that shows up at their door through the month club, whatever it might be. These recurring subscriptions are just great because they are essentially the gifts that keep on giving. All right? Now number five, I want you to remember their family. What do I mean? Well, quite simply, when you're doing your post-closing intelligence gathering, ask them questions about their family. Any sons, any daughters, what's their anniversary? What a certain birthday date that might match. The kids' birthdays, their own, whatever it might be. Find out information about their family and then use that to give your gifts. I don't always write your thank you notes, by the way. For example, at least this is right to number six, which is look for special situations. See what I mean by that is that when you know about their kids and their loved ones, you can find out when certain things are happening like weddings, confirmations, bomb mitts, whatever it might be, engagement parties and give gifts in honor of those things. Also, write your thank you notes accordingly to honor those things as well. Let me tell you something. Personally for me, when I get a personal little gift, it can be worth 30 bucks. That comes from someone that I did business with that remembered either my kids' graduation or someone's birthday or something that my wife is going through. It just makes you feel really warm and fuzzy inside of them, serious. Remember, sometimes keeping that account is going to come down to that warm, fuzzy and trusting feeling that that endures. At the end of the day, that trusted establishes the idea that you don't just want money from you, you have the best interest at heart. This is all what goes into that second 10, third 10 as well, and then give you that edge, that unconscious edge when they're calculating the emotional side of the equation for the first 10. So, as the future pays the first 10, the actual product or service, remember, you're involved in delivering that product to speak to you, do business with you. If they like you and trust you, guess what? They're going to want to do business with you. All right? Lastly, this is a very important one, check the laws. In other words, don't be giving cash gifts or expensive gifts if you're in an industry that prohibits that. Now, again, I don't know what industry you're in, but if you're a lawyer, you're going to be very careful with stuff for a doctor or probably actually if you're maybe selling pharmaceuticals and giving gifts to doctors, just be careful. So make sure that you check with your compliance department, whatever legal team is associated with your business, and just make sure you abide by A, the laws of the state and country, and also any guidelines that your own business has set up. All right? Now, let's move on to the next one here, which is you must learn to manage expectations correctly. Remember what I said? You want to what? Under promise and over deliver. Again, I've been through this. I just want to remit you to one more time here because you could do everything right and deliver great value to someone, like value that far exceeds the cost that you put on the product or service, right? Yet if you promise them even more, they hate you. It doesn't serve you. It doesn't serve the long term relationship and it will poison the long term relationship. By the way, the only one thing that poisons it even more and that is ducking phone calls. You get it? So again, don't duck phone calls and don't over promise and then duck the call when you don't deliver, right? It's like the death knell. All right? Now, number six, I want you to always leave your customer in a better place. Let me tell you what I mean by this. Sometimes and by the way, it happens very often is that you sell your customer something, but you could actually sell them more of that something. And if you do that, you'll make some extra money upfront and maybe even meet a certain quota. But all of a sudden, what went from a positive experience for the customer, they filled the need becomes a negative experience because yeah, they filled the need, but now they walk by like, here's a great example. In the old business of selling toner for copying, if you use like the ink for copiers, right? You know, these companies, they would sell you toner for your copier and they were aggressive on the phone because I got sold because I'll buy anything, right? I'm a sucker, right? When I was very young, I had someone guy sell me toner and he sold me a toner. He sold me enough toner to choke off like two years. Yeah. Now, eventually two years later, I was through the toner, but every single time I walked by that pile of toner, I want to strangle the guy if I could see him. You get it? So this is a con rule, really, is that you want to leave your customer in a better spot. It's about ethics, integrity, and that every encounter between you and your customer should leave the customer feeling empowered and in a better position. You don't want to sell them things they don't need or more of what they need. I think of the intro, I said, you know, you don't want to sell your customer a dump truck worth of soil when they come in for a bag of soil. That's all they need. It does nothing for the long-term relationship. In fact, poison is even in the short-term relationship and it is no way to build the sort of business that's going to give you massive referrals and get the bar from you again and again and again. All right? So let's stop right now. I want you to take a short quiz, go through your exercises, and as soon as you're done meet me in the next lesson, we'll go through my referral system, show you how to build a massive stream of them that goes on and on, and then we'll wrap things up after that. I'll see you soon.
